responses.text: The New York Post article by Ben Cost, published March 4, 2021 and updated March 5, 2021, frames a TikTok-driven Gen Z effort to "cancel" Eminem over the 2010 song "Love the Way You Lie" as having been rebuffed by viral millennial defenses of the rapper.

Independent reporting from NBC News, Esquire, and Know Your Meme confirms that a Gen Z TikTok controversy criticizing Eminem's lyrics about violence against women, especially the "tie her to the bed and set this house on fire" line, did go viral in February 2021 and prompted a wave of millennial defense videos.

The most-viewed defense clip the Post describes — a woman rapping "This man was around before you were here" over the "Forgot About Dre" beat — is a real, widely covered TikTok by user @cassiesmith607 that surpassed several million views in early March 2021.

The Post's biographical details about Eminem check out: Marshall Mathers was 48 in March 2021, was raised in the Detroit area, and "Love the Way You Lie" featuring Rihanna was released in 2010 on the album "Recovery."

The Post's claim that Rihanna has said the verse was a condemnation rather than a celebration of domestic abuse is broadly supported by her past interviews, in which she said the song "painfully" depicted the cycle of abuse and that she could relate because of her own experience, though she did not use the exact word "condemnation."

The article's aside that "more powerful people" including two U.S. presidents have tried and failed to cancel Eminem refers accurately to his long-running feuds with George W. Bush ("Mosh," "White America") and Donald Trump (the 2017 BET Hip Hop Awards freestyle "The Storm").

Important missing context: the Post says Eminem "has yet to weigh in," but within days of publication he did respond by releasing a lyric video for "Tone Deaf," a track from "Music to Be Murdered By – Side B" whose lyrics explicitly mock cancel-culture critics, a response covered by Variety, CNN, and Rolling Stone.

Additional missing context the Post omits: outlets including NBC News and Esquire reported that the original Gen Z "cancel Eminem" TikTok was removed and its creator faced harassment, framing the backlash less as a generational victory and more as a pile-on against a single young user.

On source quality, the New York Post is a Rupert Murdoch-owned tabloid that independent media-bias trackers rate as right-leaning with mixed factual reliability and a history of sensational framing, so its loaded language ("social-media guillotine," "haters," "zoomer") should be read as opinionated entertainment writing rather than neutral reporting.

Bottom line: the article's core, verifiable facts — the existence of the viral TikTok dispute, the song details, Rihanna's stance, and Eminem's history of high-profile feuds — are accurate, but the piece overstates a tidy generational narrative, downplays harassment of the original critic, and was overtaken within 48 hours by Eminem's own "Tone Deaf" response.
